title: Insulation options for a loft conversion
subtitle: Cold roof, warm roof, and hybrid: choosing the right configuration
excerpt: A loft conversion needs insulation at the sloping ceiling and gable walls, not at the original ceiling level. The choice between insulating between-and-below the rafters, above the rafters, or using a hybrid depends on the rafter depth, headroom, and whether the roof is being re-covered.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Approved Document L1B (2022) and Approved Document F (2021) for loft conversion thermal and ventilation requirements. TRADA Technology guidance on timber-frame insulation. NHBC Standards Chapter 7.2 on loft conversions.

content.text: When a loft is converted into a habitable room, the insulation moves from the original ceiling (the floor of the loft) to the sloping ceiling and the gable walls. The original ceiling-level insulation is removed from the affected areas. The unoccupied sides of the loft retain their ceiling-level insulation as before.
The target
U-value
for the roof is 0.18 W/m2K. For a sloping ceiling with exposed rafters, meeting this target requires
PIR board
in two positions: between the rafters and below them.
The three configurations
Between and below the rafters (standard approach)

For a typical 100 mm rafter depth: leave a 25 mm ventilation gap at the top, fill 75 mm with PIR between the rafters, then fix a continuous layer of 40 mm PIR below the rafters. This achieves approximately 0.18 W/m2K with the rafter bridging correction. For 150 mm rafters: 125 mm between the rafters plus 25 mm below gives around 0.15 W/m2K.
A
warm roof

configuration keeps the structural timber warm. The ventilation gap above the insulation is still required to drain any moisture that reaches the space below the covering. A vapour control layer below the between-rafter insulation (room side) reduces moisture movement from the interior.
Above the rafters (roof being re-covered)

If the roof covering is being replaced as part of the conversion, insulation can go above the rafters before the new covering. This avoids any headroom loss inside and eliminates the rafter bridge entirely. Structural calculations are needed to check that the rafters can carry the combined weight of insulation and new covering. The total insulation thickness above the rafters for 0.18 W/m2K is 120 mm of PIR.
Hybrid: above and below, no between-rafter layer

Where rafter depth is shallow (75 mm, common in 1950s-1970s houses) and the covering is being replaced, a hybrid approach puts 80 mm PIR above the rafters and 40 mm PIR below. This avoids stuffing a thin rafter bay with insulation and achieves better thermal performance. Structural check required.
Gable walls

Gable walls in a loft conversion are typically a single-leaf brick or block skin. The target U-value is 0.28 W/m2K for the wall. Internal wall insulation (a service cavity with mineral wool, or PIR boards on dabs) is the normal approach. The same rules apply here as for any solid wall: vapour control layer on the warm side, airtight joints at perimeters.
Ventilation

A newly used loft room adds moisture from the people in it and from any bathroom. Building Regulations Part F asks for either background ventilation (trickle vents in any new windows) or fan-based ventilation. Where a bathroom is going in, a heat-recovery system (MVHR) is often the right call. It keeps moisture moving out of a tightly insulated loft.
When to call a specialist

A loft conversion always needs Building Regulations approval. The structural sums, the fire-escape route, and the staircase width are all Building Control matters. The insulation spec should be in the drawings you submit. An energy assessor or an architectural technologist can write the thermal spec as part of those drawings.
